---
title: "Research"
title-block-banner: "#4100AB"
toc: false
page-layout: full
include-back-link: true
toc-location: right
---

I study social and behavioral processes that influence health outcomes with mixed-methods. My research is interdisciplinary and often draws upon psychology, economics, data science, sociology, communications, and gender studies within a public health framework. My research has covered populations in several countries. Aside from wide-ranging systematic reviews covering multiple countries, this map illustrates populations covered in country-specific studies.

<div id="map" style="width: 100%; position: relative;"></div>
<div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; margin-top: 12px; font-size: 12px; color: #555;">
<span style="width: 10px; height: 10px; border-radius: 2px; background: #534AB7; display:inline-block;"></span>
<span>Countries covered by my published country-specific studies</span>
</div>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/d3@7"></script>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/topojson@3"></script>
<script>
const covered = new Set(['United States of America','Colombia','Kenya','Rwanda','Oman','India','China']);
const mapDiv = document.getElementById('map');

const tooltip = document.createElement('div');
tooltip.style.cssText = 'position:fixed; pointer-events:none; background:white; border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:6px; padding:4px 10px; font-size:13px; opacity:0; transition:opacity 0.1s; z-index:10;';
mapDiv.appendChild(tooltip);

const svg = d3.select(mapDiv).append('svg').attr('viewBox', '0 0 960 500').attr('width', '100%');
const path = d3.geoPath(d3.geoNaturalEarth1().scale(155).translate([480, 260]));

d3.json('https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/world-atlas@2/countries-110m.json').then(world => {
  svg.selectAll('path').data(topojson.feature(world, world.objects.countries).features).join('path')
    .attr('d', path).attr('stroke', 'white').attr('stroke-width', 0.5)
    .attr('fill', d => covered.has(d.properties.name) ? '#534AB7' : '#D3D1C7')
    .on('mousemove', (event, d) => {
      tooltip.textContent = d.properties.name;
      tooltip.style.left = (event.clientX + 12) + 'px';
      tooltip.style.top = (event.clientY + 12) + 'px';
      tooltip.style.opacity = 1;
    })
    .on('mouseleave', () => { tooltip.style.opacity = 0; });
});
</script>

For the most up-to-date list of my publications, please see my [Google Scholar profile](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=K44Uu4MAAAAJ&hl=en).

Below, I highlight a few selected publications.


## Selected Publications

::: {.pub-grid}

::: {.pub-card}
<span class="pub-tag tag-ntr">Nicotine & Tobacco Research</span>

#### Social norms change and cigarette smoking: a longitudinal study in mainland China

<div class="authors">Lahiri, S., Bingenheimer, J.B., Wang, Y., Evans, W.D., & Cislaghi, B. (2026)</div>

**Methods:** Bayesian latent growth curve models applied to 9 years of longitudinal survey data from adult smokers across 4 Chinese cities.

**Key finding:** Stronger pro-smoking social norms — both perceptions of how common smoking is and how acceptable it is — predicted higher smoking levels over time, even after accounting for addiction severity and household smoking exposure.

[View paper →](https://doi.org/10.1093/ntr/ntag052)
:::

::: {.pub-card}
<span class="pub-tag tag-addiction">Addiction</span>

#### Understanding the mechanisms of change in social norms around tobacco use

<div class="authors">Lahiri, S., Bingenheimer, J.B., Evans, W.D., Wang, Y., Cislaghi, B., Dubey, P., & Snowden, B. (2025)</div>

**Methods:** Systematic review and meta-analysis of tobacco intervention studies, searching Scopus, PubMed, PsycINFO, and several trial registries and grey-literature sources. Use of Robust Variance Estimation meta-analytic, meta-regression, and Egger's test models.

**Key finding:** Synthesized a highly fragmented evidence base to clarify how different social norms change mechanisms (e.g., role-modeling, correcting misperceptions, resistance skills training) shift tobacco-related social norms and behavior, while identifying key gaps and reporting biases in the field. Largest meta-analysis of social norms interventions to date.

[View paper →](https://doi.org/10.1111/add.16685)
:::

::: {.pub-card}
<span class="pub-tag tag-copsyc">Current Opinion in Psychology</span>

#### Resisting harmful social norms change using social inoculation

<div class="authors">Lahiri, S. (2024)</div>

**Methods:** Conceptual synthesis applying inoculation theory — originally developed to build resistance to persuasion — to the spread of harmful social norms.

**Key finding:** Proposes social inoculation as a promising, evidence-informed strategy for helping people resist harmful norm shifts, particularly as misinformation accelerates how quickly such norms can spread online.

[View paper →](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2024.101914)
:::

::: {.pub-card}
<span class="pub-tag tag-plos">PLOS ONE</span>

#### The role of social norms on adolescent family planning in rural Kilifi county, Kenya

<div class="authors">Lahiri, S., Bingenheimer, J., Sedlander, E., Munar, W., & Rimal, R. (2023)</div>

**Methods:** Survey of youth ages 15–24 in rural Kilifi County, Kenya, who each nominated up to 5 close social contacts; associations examined via logistic regression.

**Key finding:** Peers' contraceptive use and perceived social norms around contraception were both linked to a young person's own likelihood of using modern contraception — highlighting social norms as a promising intervention target.

[View paper →](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0275824)
:::

::: {.pub-card}
<span class="pub-tag" style="background-color:#5F5E5A;">International Journal of Disaster Risk Reduction</span>

#### Multidisciplinary team processes parallel natural disaster preparedness and response: A qualitative case study

<div class="authors">Lahiri, S., Snowden, B., Gu, J., Krishnan, N., Yellin, H., & Ndiaye, K. (2021)</div>

**Methods:** Semi-structured interviews with members of a multidisciplinary university research initiative on natural disaster preparedness, analyzed as a qualitative case study.

**Key finding:** Identified specific facilitators and barriers to cross-disciplinary collaboration at the outset of a research initiative, along with team members' attitudes and expectations about working across disciplines.

[View paper →](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijdrr.2021.102369)
:::

::: {.pub-card}
<span class="pub-tag" style="background-color:#993556;">Prevention Science</span>

#### Improved relationship quality, equitable gender attitudes, and reduced alcohol abuse as key mechanisms to reduce intimate partner violence in the Bandebereho couples' randomized trial in Rwanda

<div class="authors">Levtov, R.G., Doyle, K., Bingenheimer, J.B., Lahiri, S., Kazimbaya, S., Karamage, E., Sayinzoga, F., Mutoni, M., Rubayita, C.H., & Barker, G. (2022)</div>

**Methods:** Structural equation modeling using two follow-up rounds of data from a randomized controlled trial of the Bandebereho couples' intervention in Rwanda, testing hypothesized pathways from treatment to reduced intimate partner violence.

**Key finding:** Men's reduced alcohol use, more frequent communication, greater emotional closeness, less quarreling, and more equitable gender attitudes all improved significantly among intervention couples — identifying the specific mechanisms through which the program reduced violence.

[View paper →](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-022-01445-6)
:::

:::
